title: Don't disable selinux labels if user specifies a security opt
body:
Currenty if the user specifies --pid=host or --ipc=host or --privileged
then we disable SELinux labeling. If the user however specifies
--security-opt label:...  Then we assume they want to leave SELinux enabled
and know what they are doing.

This PR will leave SELinux enabled if a user specifies a --security-opt label
option.

Signed-off-by: Daniel J Walsh <dwalsh@redhat.com>

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *rhatdan* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](?repo=containers%2Flibpod). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/v1.6/OWNERS)~~ [rhatdan] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

Hello!
Thank you for this patch - I'm testing it right now, and see it will force a new change on our side:
IF we pass ```--privileged --pid=host```   THEN it disable selinux as expected.
IF we pass ```--privileged --pid=host --security-opt label=....```  THEN it will NOT disable selinux and just apply the labels.

This means we'll need to ensure we actually do pass ```--security-opt label=type:spc_t``` when we pass the "--pid=host". Not a big deal, but it's something we have to do, right?

For instance:
```
podman run --rm --privileged --net=host --pid=host docker.io/library/centos:8 cat /proc/self/attr/current
unconfined_u:system_r:spc_t:s0

podman run --rm --privileged --net=host --pid=host --security-opt label=level:s0 docker.io/library/centos:8 cat /proc/self/attr/current
system_u:system_r:container_t:s0

podman run --rm --privileged --net=host --pid=host --security-opt label=level:s0 --security-opt label=type:spc_t docker.io/library/centos:8 cat /proc/self/attr/current
system_u:system_r:spc_t:s0
```
This would ensure the process is actually running as privileged.

Do you confirm this is the intended behaviour? If so, I think it would be fine, as long as we do the edition job on our side.

Cheers,

C.

Let's put it this way.  If you set the --security-opt label=* flag, then SELinux will be enabled, bottom line.
Currently 
--privileged, --ipc=host and --pid=host disable SELinux separation.  The reason we do this, is most applications will be blocked from access to the different types exposed by the --ipc=host and --pid=host flags.  If the user specifies --security-opt label=* then we assume that they understand how SELinux works and will do the right thing with the flags.

Bottom line is yes if you set the --secuirty-opt label=* and want SELinux to not interface with container separation, then  you MUST set the spc_t type --security-opt label=type:spc_t

Thank you Dan for the details! After the first test, I can confirm this last patch seems to solve our issues, and, nice addition, we apparently don't need to do more changes on our side.
Thank you again!
I'm going to go ahead and merge - we can land further changes in additional PRs if we need to
